metadata.title: Guideline on quality, non-clinical and clinical aspects of medicinal products containing genetically modified cells
metadata.document_type: Final Guideline — adopted by CHMP 12 November 2020, in effect 1 June 2021
metadata.note: This Rev.1 supersedes the original 2012 version (came into effect 1 November 2012). No prior extraction found in output-v2/v3/v4. Covers quality, non-clinical, and clinical requirements for GMC-containing medicinal products including CAR-T, TCR, iPS-derived, and genome-edited cell products.

rules.rule_text: For ex vivo gene transfer, principles of Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) shall apply from the bank system used to produce the vector (viral/non-viral vector, mRNA, proteins) onwards.
rules.verbatim_quote: For ex vivo gene transfer, the tools used to genetically modify the cells shall be, as appropriate, the vector (e.g. viral or non-viral vector) and the components to produce them. Principles of good manufacturing practice (GMP) shall apply from the bank system used to produce the vector onwards.
rules.exceptions: For iPS cell generation, GMP and scientific recommendations apply after procurement including generation and subsequent selection process.

rules.rule_text: For combined ATMPs containing genetically modified cells, additional substances (scaffolds, matrices, devices, biomaterials, biomolecules and/or other components) which form an integral part of the product shall be considered starting materials and must be qualified for their intended use.
rules.verbatim_quote: For combined ATMP containing genetically modified cells, additional substances (e.g. scaffolds, matrices, devices, biomaterials, biomolecules and/or other components) which are combined with the manipulated cells, of which they form an integral part, shall be considered as starting materials, even if not of biological origin (definition as laid down in 2009/120/EC directive). They should be qualified for their intended use as recommended in the guideline on human cell-based medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/410869/2006).

rules.rule_text: Starting materials used for genetically modified cells and genome-edited products shall be carefully qualified to assure a consistent manufacturing process. Information on starting materials must be included in the CTD under 'control of materials'.
rules.verbatim_quote: Starting materials used for the production of genetically modified cells and genome edited products shall be carefully qualified to assure a consistent manufacturing process. [...] Information on starting materials should be included in the Common Technical Document (CTD) under the heading of "control of materials", either when produced in house or supplied by another manufacturer.
rules.exceptions: For the vector and cells separate 3.2.S modules can be considered.

rules.rule_text: The type of delivery vector/vehicle used for ex vivo genetic modification must be justified based on the target cells, the expected genomic modification, the clinical indication, etc. When using integrating vectors, a Self-Inactivating (SIN) vector design is recommended to reduce insertional mutagenesis risk.
rules.verbatim_quote: The type of delivery vector or vehicle used for ex vivo genetic modification should be justified based on the target cells, the expected genomic modification, the clinical indication, etc. The molecular design of the transfer vector should be driven by safety and efficacy criteria. When using integrating vectors, an appropriate design to reduce the risks deriving from insertional mutagenesis and to increase vector safety (e.g. Self-Inactivating (SIN) vectors) is recommended.

rules.rule_text: For CRISPR/Cas9 and other genome editing nucleases, strategies to increase on-target and reduce off-target effects are required and must be justified, including transient expression of the nuclease and appropriate design of DNA binding domains and guide RNA.
rules.verbatim_quote: Likewise, when genome editing nucleases such as CRISPR/Cas9 are expressed in target cells, strategies to increase on-target and reduce off-target effects are required and should be justified. These include transient expression of the nuclease and an appropriate design of the encoded DNA binding domains of the modifying enzyme and of the small guide RNA to increase the modifying enzyme selectivity.

rules.rule_text: Prior to use, the transfer vector must be sterile and shown to be free from unwanted viral contamination including helper or hybrid viruses, adventitious contamination, and replication-competent vectors. A validated, sensitive assay (e.g. quantitative PCR complemented with infectivity assay) must be used; non-purified transfer vectors must not be used in the transduction process.
rules.verbatim_quote: Prior to its use, the transfer vector is expected to be sterile and should be shown to be free from any unwanted viral contamination, including helper or hybrid viruses such as in AAV production systems, adventitious contamination or replication-competent vectors for vectors intended to be replication deficient. For the latter, a validated, sensitive assay (or combination of assays), such as a quantitative PCR assay complemented with an infectivity assay in permissive cells, should be used. Use of non-purified transfer vectors in the transduction process should be avoided.

rules.rule_text: The use of unrelated DNA sequences, such as selection markers, that can end up in the final genetically modified cells should be avoided unless justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of unrelated DNA sequences, such as selection markers, that can end up in the final genetically modified cells should be avoided unless justified.

rules.rule_text: All procedures for any manipulation during manufacturing must be documented in detail and closely monitored according to specified process controls including process parameters, operating ranges, in-process controls/tests, and materials' attributes.
rules.verbatim_quote: The procedures for any manipulation should be documented in detail and closely monitored according to specified process controls (including process parameters and operating ranges, in-process controls/tests and materials' attributes).

rules.rule_text: Replication competent virus (RCV) testing as an in-process test is not deemed necessary if absence of RCV has been demonstrated at the level of the viral vector starting material using validated and sensitive assay(s), and RCV formation during manufacturing can be excluded. A risk assessment must be presented to address potential generation of RCVs during manufacturing.
rules.verbatim_quote: Replication competent virus (RCV) testing as an in-process test is not deemed necessary, provided that absence of RCV has been demonstrated (using validated and sensitive assay(s)) at the level of the viral vector starting material and that RCV formation during manufacturing of the genetically modified cells can be excluded. In this case, a risk assessment should be presented to address the potential generation of RCVs during manufacturing.
rules.exceptions: RCV testing at in-process level can be waived if conditions above are met.

rules.rule_text: A clear definition of a production batch (from cell sourcing and vector) must be provided for labelling of the final container, including batch size, number of cell passages/cell duplications, pooling strategies, and batch numbering system.
rules.verbatim_quote: A clear definition of a production batch (from cell sourcing and vector) used for labelling of the final container should be provided (i.e. size, number of cell passages/cell duplications, pooling strategies, batch numbering system).

rules.rule_text: Full details of all process parameters and in-process tests with corresponding numeric operating range/set point and acceptance criteria/action limits must be provided to ensure the desired product Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs).
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, full details of all process parameters and in-process tests and corresponding numeric operating range/set point and acceptance criteria/action limits to ensure the desired product critical quality attributes (CQAs) should be provided.

rules.rule_text: Genetic modification must be carried out using a validated manufacturing process. When using integrating vectors (e.g. LV and RV), multiplicity of infection (MOI) must be kept at the minimum shown to be effective by transduction efficiency studies and clinical studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: Genetic modification should be carried out using a validated manufacturing process. When using integrating vectors (e.g. LV and RV), multiplicity of infection should be kept at the minimum shown to be effective by transduction efficiency studies and clinical studies.

rules.rule_text: For genome editing protocols, generation of on- and off-target modifications must be addressed as part of process development and characterisation. A risk assessment must be presented to address the potential appearance of off-target modifications during manufacturing.
rules.verbatim_quote: For genome editing protocols, generation of on- and off-target modifications should be addressed as part of process development and characterisation. A risk assessment should be presented to address the potential appearance of off-target modifications during manufacturing.

rules.rule_text: For bankable genetically modified cells, a cell bank system must be established and controlled according to applicable guidelines (Ph. Eur. 5.2.3, ICH Q5D).
rules.verbatim_quote: For bankable genetically modified cells, a cell bank system should be established and controlled according to the principles detailed in applicable guidelines (e.g. Ph. Eur. 5.2.3, ICH Q5D).

rules.rule_text: Process validation for genetically modified cells must address: absence of adventitious contaminants, absence of modifying enzymes and nucleic acids, removal of infectious particles, remaining free vector, transduction/transfection efficiency, VCN, transgene identity and integrity, level of transgene expression, structure and function of expressed molecule(s), removal/elimination of target nucleic acid sequences, and removal/reduction of impurities associated with the genetic modification.
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to the requirements described for process validation in the Guideline on human cell-based medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/410869/2006), the following aspects should be addressed, as applicable: absence of adventitious contaminants, absence of modifying enzymes and nucleic acids, removal of infectious particles, remaining free vector, transduction/transfection efficiency, vector copy number, transgene identity and integrity (and of other regions as needed), level of transgene expression, structure and function of the expressed molecule(s), removal or elimination of the target nucleic acid sequences when appropriate, removal or reduction of impurities associated with the genetic modification.
rules.exceptions: Limited cell/tissue availability may challenge validation; reduced process validation must be offset by additional in-process testing.

rules.rule_text: All changes introduced during development must be clearly identified within the dossier and appropriate comparability studies must be conducted to compare pre- and post-change product and assess the impact of any observed difference on quality, safety, and efficacy.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is important that all changes introduced during development are clearly identified within the dossier. In addition, appropriate comparability studies are needed in order to: i) compare pre- and post-change product and ii) assess the impact of any observed difference on the quality attributes as it relates to safety and efficacy of the product.

rules.rule_text: For high-risk manufacturing process changes (e.g. manufacturing site change), comparability between pre- and post-change products must include release tests, relevant stability studies, extended characterisation, and in-process controls, as well as any other relevant process parameters.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2.6.3 Changes in the active substance/finished product manufacturing process
rules.verbatim_quote: For changes concluded to have a high risk, such as a manufacturing site change, comparability between pre- and post-change products should include release tests, relevant stability studies, extended characterisation and in-process controls as well as any other relevant process parameter.

rules.rule_text: Rigorous characterisation of the genetically modified cell product is essential and must cover the following parameters using appropriately qualified molecular, biological, and immunological methods: cell identity/viability, phenotype/morphology, heterogeneity of cell population, proliferation/differentiation capacity, transduction/transfection efficiency, sequence and integrity of transgene, genetic stability upon in vitro proliferation/differentiation, identity and activity of expressed gene product, VCN per transduced/transfected cell, vector integration profile, persistence of genome editing tools, and on-target and off-target genetic modifications.
rules.verbatim_quote: The use of a range of appropriately qualified molecular, biological and immunological methods for the following characteristics should be addressed, as appropriate: cell identity and viability; cell phenotype / morphology; heterogeneity of the cell population (e.g. percentage of sub-populations); proliferation and/or differentiation capacity of the genetically modified cells; cell functionality (other than proliferation/differentiation, when applicable); transduction/transfection efficiency (e.g. percentage of transduced cells); sequence and integrity of transgene; genetic stability upon in vitro proliferation and/or differentiation; identity and activity of the expressed gene product; vector copy number per transduced/transfected cell; vector integration profile (when applicable); vector/transgenes removal or elimination (when applicable); vector release from cells; vector replication competence and possibility of reactivation [...]; persistence of genome editing tools in the cells; on-target and off-target genetic modifications.

rules.rule_text: The vector copy number (VCN) per transduced/transfected cell must be justified in relation to the safety data and the intended use. The integration profile of integrating vectors or plasmids should be studied in relation to known oncogenes/tumour suppressor genes.
rules.verbatim_quote: The vector copy number per transduced/transfected cell should be justified in relation to the safety data and the intended use of the product. To address the risk deriving from insertional mutagenesis, the integration profile of integrating vectors or plasmids should be studied in relation to known oncogenes/tumour suppressor genes, where applicable.
rules.exceptions: Post-administration screening of vector integration in patients could supplement pre-release integration profile data.

rules.rule_text: For cells modified using genome-editing tools, induced off-target changes must be identified using at least one sensitive and well-characterised experimental assay in the cell type to be used therapeutically or in surrogate settings, plus appropriate bioinformatics tools for in silico screening. Candidate genomic sites must then be interrogated by deep-sequencing (or equivalent) in the actual cell type.
rules.verbatim_quote: For cells modified using genome-editing tools, induced off-target changes should be identified using at least one sensitive and well-characterized experimental assay in the cell type to be used therapeutically or in surrogate settings (e.g. healthy donor cells). In addition, appropriate bioinformatics tools for in silico screening are expected. [...] This set of candidate genomic sites should then be interrogated by deep-sequencing (or other appropriate technique) in the actual cell type to be used therapeutically and treated according to the proposed protocol and nuclease expression level/dose.
rules.exceptions: In silico screening alone is insufficient — experimental unbiased genome-wide evaluation is required.

rules.rule_text: Identity testing for release must include assays to detect both the specific cell population AND the intended genetic modification (at DNA level or assay detecting the intended product translated from the genetic modification at the protein level).
rules.verbatim_quote: Identity testing should include assays to detect the presence of the specific cell population as well as the intended genetic modification (at DNA level or an assay to detect the presence of the intended product translated from the genetic modification at the protein level). The test methods should be specific for those components.

rules.rule_text: Purity criteria must be established within specified limits. Tests must determine levels of cellular impurities including other cell types, unintendedly modified, non-transduced/non-transfected or unmodified genome-edited target cells, and cell fragments. Non-cellular impurities added during production must also be tested.
rules.verbatim_quote: The purity criteria should be established and be within specified limits. Tests should be applied to determine levels of cellular impurities such as other cell types including those unintendedly modified, non-transduced/non-transfected or unmodified genome edited target cells and cell fragments. In addition, non-cellular impurities materials which may have been added during the production processes should be tested for.

rules.rule_text: When a viral vector is used for transduction, the level of infectious particles in the final product must be determined and kept below a justified limit. For transposon vectors, it must be shown that the final cell population is free of transposase activity.
rules.verbatim_quote: When a viral vector is used for transduction, the level of infectious particles in the final product should be determined and kept below a justified limit. When using transposon vectors it should be shown that the final cell population is free of transposase activity.

rules.rule_text: For replication-deficient viral vectors, tests to show the absence of replication-competent viruses (RCV) are essential; however, if absence of RCV is demonstrated at other levels (e.g. viral vector starting material), no additional testing is required provided that RCV generation during manufacturing is ruled out by risk assessment. The RCV assay must have an appropriate limit of detection justified by a risk assessment considering the worst case for a human dose.
rules.verbatim_quote: In the case of replication deficient viral vectors, tests to show the absence of replication-competent viruses (RCV) are essential; however, if absence of RCV is demonstrated at other levels (for instance at the viral vector starting material) no additional testing is required provided that generation of RCVs during manufacturing is ruled out by an appropriate risk assessment. The assay for RCV should have an appropriate limit of detection, justified in the risk assessment taking into consideration the worst case and expressed for a human dose.
rules.exceptions: Omission of final product RCV testing is justified if demonstrated at vector release level with validated, sensitive assay.

rules.rule_text: Biological potency tests must be applied to determine the functional properties of the genetically modified cells and those achieved by the genetic modification. Potency tests must provide quantitative information on cell function and transgene product, and a reference batch with assigned potency must be established.
rules.verbatim_quote: To estimate the potency of the genetically modified cells, biological tests should be applied to determine the functional properties of the cells, where applicable, and those achieved by the genetic modification. The potency test(s) should provide, as far as possible, quantitative information on the intended function of the cell and the transgene product. [...] Wherever possible, a reference batch of cells with assigned potency should be established and used to calibrate tests.
rules.exceptions: Biological potency tests in animal tissues maintained ex vivo or whole animals only acceptable where suitable in vitro method cannot be developed.

rules.rule_text: Potency testing for CAR-T cell products must preferably be based on the cytotoxic potential of the T-cells against tumour cells (e.g. actual death of target tumour cells, induction of intracellular pathways leading to irreversible target cell death). Surrogate read-outs may be used provided their relation with target cell death is shown.
rules.verbatim_quote: Potency testing for products containing genetically modified T-cells against tumour cells (e.g. CAR-T cells) is preferably based on the cytotoxic potential of the T-cells. Assay read-outs could, therefore, include actual death of target tumour cells or induction of intracellular pathways and loss of membrane integrity (with leakage of intracellular components) shown to lead to irreversible target cell death. Surrogate read-outs for biological activity of CAR-T cell products could be the secretion of specific cytokines/cytotoxic molecules or expression of activation/degranulation markers by T-cells, provided that relation with target cell death is shown.
rules.exceptions: When no autologous tumour material can be used as target, the relevance of surrogate target cells must be justified.

rules.rule_text: Release testing must include analysis of quantity, identity, purity, impurities (product- and process-related), and potency. The VCN of integrated vectors per transduced or transfected cell must be tested on each batch of final product as a read-out for safety and potency.
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to general pharmaceutical tests (e.g. sterility, endotoxin, appearance etc.), release testing should include analysis of quantity, identity, purity, impurities (product- and process-related) and potency. [...] The copy number of integrated vectors per transduced or transfected cell as read-out for safety and potency should be tested on each batch of final product.
rules.exceptions: Two-step release testing may be conducted in exceptional, well-justified cases (some data available only post-administration); missing first-step information must be compensated by appropriate in-process testing and extensive process validation.

rules.rule_text: For cells transduced with a replication-defective vector, the absence of RCV must be demonstrated before clinical use. Omission of RCV analysis at final product level may be justified in the absence of RCV at vector release using a validated, sensitive assay.
rules.verbatim_quote: For cells transduced with a replication defective vector, the absence of RCV should be demonstrated before clinical use. Depending on the risk of RCV formation, omission of analysis for RCV at final product level could be justified in case absence of RCV is confirmed at vector release using a validated, sensitive assay (or combination of assays).
rules.exceptions: Risk-based justification required for omission at final product level.

rules.rule_text: Stability studies (including in-use stability) must be conducted according to the principles described in the Guideline on human cell-based medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/410869/2006). Quality attributes followed during stability must be stability-indicating and quantitative and able to detect clinically meaningful changes.
rules.verbatim_quote: Stability studies, including in-use stability studies, should be conducted according to the principles described in the Guideline on human cell-based medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/410869/2006). Quality attributes to be followed during stability studies should be defined on the basis of characterisation studies. They should be stability indicating (and quantitative) and be able to detect clinically meaningful changes in the product.

rules.rule_text: Reconstitution activities (after batch release, prior to administration) must be justified as not performable as part of the manufacturing process before batch release, with no negative impact on the product. No activity entailing substantial manipulation (e.g. cultivation) can be considered reconstitution.
rules.verbatim_quote: For each step of the reconstitution process it has to be justified that it cannot be performed as part of the manufacturing process before batch release without negative impact on the product. No activity that entails substantial manipulation can, however, be considered reconstitution (e.g. cultivation).
rules.exceptions: Reconstitution activities may be performed at the administration site (e.g. hospital pharmacies) outside a GMP environment.

rules.rule_text: Non-clinical studies must be performed in relevant in vitro, ex vivo, and animal models in light of the target cell population, clinical indication, and route of administration. The 3Rs principles (replacement, reduction, refinement) must be considered; animal testing should be replaced by in vitro or ex vivo studies where appropriate.
rules.verbatim_quote: The non-clinical studies should be performed in relevant in vitro, ex vivo and animal models in light of the target cell population, clinical indication and route of administration. When necessary, in vivo animal studies should be carefully planned to ensure generation of robust data while considering the 3Rs (reduction, replacement, refinement) principles. Any animal testing resulting in inconclusive data should be avoided.
rules.exceptions: Where appropriate, animal testing should be replaced by in vitro or ex vivo studies (2D/3D tissue models, organoids, microfluidics, in silico models).

rules.rule_text: Proof-of-concept studies either supporting the potential clinical effect and/or proving the anticipated mode of action must be provided. If in vivo proof-of-concept is not feasible in animal models, demonstration of scientific rationale by in vitro target-specific mode of action is appropriate.
rules.verbatim_quote: Proof-of-concept studies that either support the potential clinical effect and/or prove the anticipated mode of action should be provided. It is acknowledged that it may not be feasible to demonstrate in vivo proof-of-concept of a genetically modified cell product in animal models. [...] demonstration of the scientific rationale by in vitro target-specific mode of action would be appropriate.
rules.exceptions: In vitro PoC acceptable when in vivo PoC is not feasible (e.g. target antigen expressed differently across pathophysiological conditions).

rules.rule_text: Pharmacokinetics studies must be designed to address the in vivo fate (biodistribution, homing, engraftment, stability, and persistence) of the genetically modified cells. For secreted gene products, the local and/or systemic exposure and persistence of the transgene product must be included in the analysis.
rules.verbatim_quote: Pharmacokinetics studies should be designed in order to address the in vivo fate (biodistribution, homing, engraftment, stability and persistence) of the genetically modified cells as appropriate. [...] For secreted gene products the local and/or systemic exposure and persistence of the transgene product should be included in the analysis.

rules.rule_text: For GMC products, the following toxicity considerations specific to genetic modification must be addressed in toxicology studies: toxicity related to expression of the transgene, risk of insertional mutagenesis, vector mobilisation and recombination, and product class-specific aspects (CAR/TCR T-cells, NK cells, iPS cells, ex vivo gene-edited cells).
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, the following considerations should be addressed for genetically modified cells: toxicity related to the expression of a transgene; risk of insertional mutagenesis; vector mobilisation and recombination; aspects related to specific product classes such as immune cells (CAR and TCR modified T-cells, NK cells), induced pluripotent stem cells (iPS cells), and ex vivo gene edited cells.

rules.rule_text: When cells are transduced with integrating vectors (gamma-retroviral or lentiviral), the risk of insertional oncogenesis must be carefully evaluated in accordance with the Reflection paper on management of clinical risks deriving from insertional mutagenesis (EMA/CAT/190186/2012). For a GM clonal cell line, integration at critical sites (near proto-oncogenes) must be avoided and integration site(s) must be demonstrated to not induce insertional oncogenesis.
rules.verbatim_quote: When cells are transduced with integrating vectors (e.g. gamma-retroviral or lentiviral), the risk of insertional oncogenesis needs to be carefully evaluated in accordance with the Reflection paper on management of clinical risks deriving from insertional mutagenesis (EMA/CAT/190186/2012). [...] For a genetically modified clonal cell line, the site(s) of vector integration should be determined and any vector integration at critical sites (e.g. near proto-oncogenes) should be avoided. Moreover, the integration site(s) should be demonstrated to not induce insertional oncogenesis, unless otherwise justified.
rules.exceptions: For autologous/allogeneic non-clonal cell populations: risk may need to be monitored in clinical studies by frequent insertion site analyses and clonality of patients' cells after treatment.

rules.rule_text: For CAR and TCR modified immune cells, on-target/off-tumour and off-target toxicities must be addressed using appropriate animal models or alternative approaches combining in silico and in vitro analyses. Investigation of target antigen expression in human organs, tissues, and cells is required.
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 Product class-specific considerations — Immune cells (CAR and TCR modified T-cells, NK T-cells)
rules.verbatim_quote: In case of CAR and TCR modified immune cells potential on-target/off-tumour and off-target toxicities need to be addressed as far as possible either in an appropriate animal model or by an alternative approach using a combination of in silico and in vitro analyses. [...] Investigation of expression of the target antigen is usually performed by analysing cells and tissues from healthy individuals.
rules.exceptions: Alternative approach (in silico + in vitro) indicated for TCR modified immune cells and CARs containing scFv that only recognizes human epitope.

rules.rule_text: For TCR modified T-cells: potential mispairing between introduced TCR chains and endogenous TCRs must be addressed. An adequate HLA allo-reactivity screen must be performed to obtain information on potential cross-reactivity of the TCR with other HLA alleles.
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 Product class-specific considerations — Immune cells
rules.verbatim_quote: For TCR modified T-cells, potential mispairing between the introduced TCR chains and the endogenous TCRs need to be addressed. Strategies implemented in the design of the introduced TCR chains to reduce potential mispairing needs to be described and justified. [...] In order to obtain information on potential cross-reactivity of the TCR with other HLA alleles, an adequate HLA allo-reactivity screen needs to be performed.
rules.exceptions: The chosen strategy may be adapted to the expected likelihood for cross-reactivity of the TCR.

rules.rule_text: For iPS cell-derived cell-based products: the inherent risk of tumourigenicity (iPS cells form teratomas in vivo) must be addressed per EMA/CAT/571134/2009. The non-clinical qualification of the level of undifferentiated iPS cell impurities and the risk of tumour potential must be addressed.
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 Product class-specific considerations — Cell-based products derived from induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells
rules.verbatim_quote: iPS cells carry an inherent risk of tumourigenicity as they form teratomas in vivo. Reference is made to the Reflection paper on stem cell-based medicinal products (EMA/CAT/571134/2009) about the control of the manufacturing process and non-clinical testing strategies to address the pluripotency-related risk of tumourigenicity. The non-clinical qualification of the level of undifferentiated iPS cell impurities can be addressed in an in vivo study by e.g. spiking the administered cell product with undifferentiated iPS cells in different quantities.
rules.exceptions: A suicide mechanism may mitigate the tumourigenic risk; its functionality must be confirmed in vivo.

rules.rule_text: For genome-edited cell-based products, the specificity of the modifying enzyme activity or guide RNA for the targeted genomic sequence must be confirmed in vitro, including unbiased genome-wide evaluation of off-target activity in vitro (in silico alone is insufficient). Effects of genome editing on cell phenotype and physiological functions must be analysed where indicated.
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 Product class-specific considerations — Cell-based products derived from genome editing
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to the common requirements for genetically modified cells the following aspects need to be addressed for genome edited cells: the specificity of the modifying enzyme activity or guide RNA for the targeted genomic sequence needs to be confirmed in vitro by evaluating on-target and off-target editing in relevant cells. While prediction of potential off-target activity may include in silico analysis, the chosen strategy for addressing off-target activity should also include an unbiased genome-wide evaluation of off-target activity in vitro.

rules.rule_text: Clinical trials for GMC products must be designed to allow benefit/risk assessment based on the specific characteristics of the product, the target population, and existing treatments. Distinctive features (complexity, lack of relevant animal model, immunogenicity, malignant transformation risk, need for long-term follow-up, administration procedures) must be taken into account in trial design.
rules.verbatim_quote: The clinical trials should be designed to allow a benefit/risk assessment, based on the specific characteristics of the product (transduced cells), the target population (case-by-case) and existing treatments. [...] These distinctive features have an impact on the trial design, dose selection, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics/biodistribution.

rules.rule_text: The dose of genetically modified cells is defined by the number of cells per kg, m² body surface, or as a flat dose. A starting dose assessment must identify a dose expected to have pharmacological effect and be safe; the VCN, proportion of transduced cells, engraftment, in vivo VCN, and transgene expression data must be explored to establish an effective dose range.
rules.verbatim_quote: The dose is defined by the number of genetically modified cells per kg, m² body surface or as a flat dose, as appropriate. The goal of selecting a starting dose is to identify a dose that is expected to have a pharmacological effect and is safe to use. [...] The relationship between vector copy number (VCN) and proportion of transduced cells with engraftment level, in vivo VCN, transgene expression and clinical data should then be explored to determine their safe and effective range.
rules.exceptions: For CD34+ positive GM cells, starting dose rationale based on totality of data is accepted.

rules.rule_text: Cellular kinetics, biodistribution, and persistence of genetically modified cells must be assessed clinically, as well as the level of transgene production in target and non-target tissues. Immunogenicity testing must be conducted throughout development.
rules.verbatim_quote: However, the cellular kinetics, biodistribution and persistence of genetically modified cells well as the level of the transgene production in the target and non-target tissues need to be assessed. [...] An immune response to the cells and/or the transgene product may compromise efficacy and have an impact on safety, also in cases of single administration. Thus, the immunogenicity testing should be conducted throughout the development.
rules.exceptions: Conventional ADME studies are usually not relevant for cells.

rules.rule_text: In pivotal trials of autologous genetically modified cell products, all patients enrolled with the intention to initiate treatment (who have been randomized in a controlled trial or who have signed informed consent in a single-arm trial) must be included in the primary efficacy analysis (ITT principle).
rules.verbatim_quote: In trials of autologous genetically modified cell products that are pivotal for benefit risk assessment, all patients all patients that have been enrolled with the intention to initiate treatment, e.g. who have been randomized in a randomized controlled trial or who have signed informed consent in a single-arm trial should be included in the primary efficacy analysis.
rules.exceptions: Supportive analyses can be defined for apheresed population, lymphodepleted population, or treated/infused population if well justified.

rules.rule_text: The safety database must be large enough to detect relevant short- and medium-term adverse events. The risk of delayed adverse reactions must be assessed considering the vector risk profile, gene modification nature, life-span of modified cells, biodistribution, and potential effects on developing organs. Special risk for delayed effects of integrated vectors (oncogenesis, immunogenicity, vector reactivation) must be considered for GM stem/progenitor cells.
rules.verbatim_quote: The safety database should be large enough to detect relevant short- and medium-term adverse events that may be associated with the use and/or application procedure of the genetically modified cells and enabling a meaningful benefit risk assessment. [...] In relation to a possible life-long persistence of genetically modified stem or progenitor cells, special risk for delayed effects associated with the integrated vector and its expressed products should be considered (e.g. oncogenesis, immunogenicity or vector reactivation).

rules.rule_text: The possibility that transduced cells release vector or plasmid in vivo must be investigated. The design and extent of such investigations shall depend on the properties of the construct and the outcome of non-clinical studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: The possibility that transduced cells, intentionally designed for this purpose or not, release any vector or plasmid in vivo should be investigated. The design and extent of such investigations shall depend on the properties of the construct and the outcome of the non-clinical studies.

rules.rule_text: Clinical follow-up of patients enrolled in clinical trials with genetically modified cells must be ensured according to EMEA/CHMP/GTWP/60436/2007. A 15-year follow-up is recommended according to current knowledge. If there is a risk of late onset adverse event (e.g. leukaemia, secondary malignancies, tumourigenicity), measures must be put in place.
rules.verbatim_quote: The clinical follow-up of patients enrolled in clinical trials with genetically modified cells should be ensured according to the principles laid down in the Guideline on follow-up of patients administered with gene therapy medicinal products (EMEA/CHMP/GTWP/60436/2007) [...] According to the current knowledge, a 15 year follow-up is recommended. If there is a risk of late onset of an adverse event (such as development of leukaemia or other secondary malignancies, or an identified risk of tumourigenicity on a mechanistic basis), measures have to be put in place to address this risk.

rules.rule_text: For genetically modified cells, the EU Risk Management Plan (RMP) must be prepared per EMEA/149995/2008. Specific long-term studies may be needed to monitor safety issues including lack of efficacy and risk of vector dissemination or reactivation.
rules.verbatim_quote: For genetically modified cells, the EU Risk Management Plan (RMP) requirements are described in the Guideline on safety and efficacy follow-up and risk management of Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (EMEA/149995/2008). Genetically modified cells may need specific long-term studies to monitor safety issues including lack of efficacy and risk of vector dissemination or reactivation.

rules.rule_text: The long-term safety issues for genetically modified cells — infections, immunogenicity/immunosuppression, malignant transformation, durability of associated medical device/biomaterial component — must be addressed in the Risk Management Plan. Specific pharmaco-epidemiological studies may be needed.
rules.verbatim_quote: The long-term safety issues, such as infections, immunogenicity/immunosuppression and malignant transformation as well as the durability of the associated medical device/biomaterial component should be addressed in the Risk Management Plan. Specific pharmaco-epidemiological studies may be needed.

rules.rule_text: For CAR-T cells in haemato-oncology, confirmatory trials should generally follow a randomized controlled design comparing CAR-T cell treatment to a reference regimen. The ITT principle must be applied to main efficacy trials. DFS/EFS, PFS, OS, and HR-QoL are acceptable primary efficacy endpoints in randomized controlled trials; ORR and duration of response are acceptable in exploratory trials without parallel control.
rules.source_section: Annex I — CAR-T cells in haemato-oncology: Efficacy
rules.verbatim_quote: For CAR-T cells in haemato-oncology the same basic principles to demonstrate efficacy apply as for other anticancer medicinal products. Confirmatory trials and a comprehensive safety database should aim to establish the benefit-risk profile of the product in a well-defined patient population, based on clinical benefit endpoints. A randomized controlled design is generally considered most convincing to establish efficacy. [...] DFS/EFS, PFS, OS, and health-related quality of life (HR-QoL) are considered generally acceptable primary efficacy endpoints in randomized controlled trials. Objective response rate (ORR) and duration of response are generally acceptable measures of antitumour activity in exploratory trials that do not use a parallel control.
rules.exceptions: Non-parallel controlled single arm design may be acceptable for marketing authorisation where: treatment effect can be clearly attributed, mechanism of action is well understood, clear evidence of clinical benefit and acceptable toxicity, natural course of disease is highly predictable, and unmet medical need exists.

rules.rule_text: For CAR-T cell pharmacokinetics, cellular kinetics including CAR-T cell levels and expansion/persistence in blood and target tissues at relevant time points must be characterised. In vivo cell kinetics parameters including AUCd28, Cmax, Tmax, and T1/2 must be assessed using appropriate bioanalytical methods (e.g. qPCR for CAR-specific transgene and flow cytometry).
rules.source_section: Annex I — CAR-T cells in haemato-oncology: Pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics and dose finding
rules.verbatim_quote: The pharmacokinetics of CAR-T cells performed as a part of exploratory clinical studies should characterise the cellular kinetics including CAR-T-cell levels and their expansion and persistence in blood and target tissues at relevant time points. Assessment of in vivo cell kinetics should include relevant parameters like AUCd28, Cmax, Tmax, and T1/2 using appropriate bioanalytical methods, e.g. qPCR for quantification of the CAR specific transgene and flow cytometry, to quantify CAR T cells in blood and other target tissues.
rules.exceptions: Conventional drug-drug interaction studies in renal/hepatic impairment are less applicable and considered on a case-by-case basis.
